content.text: When someone installs Java to run an application—without any intention of writing code—they install the Java Runtime Environment. The JRE is the minimum software package needed to execute a compiled Java program: it bundles the JVM with the standard class libraries that Java programs depend on.
The Java Runtime Environment (JRE) is a software layer that sits between compiled Java bytecode and the underlying operating system. It consists of two major components:
— the execution engine that loads, verifies, and runs bytecode (described in detail in the JVM topic).
— the standard API that almost every Java program relies on:
Historically (Java 8 and earlier) the JRE was a distinct, separately downloadable package—smaller than the full JDK. Starting with Java 9, the
with a set of named modules. Oracle stopped distributing a standalone JRE installer from Java 11 onward; instead, developers use
to assemble a custom minimal runtime image containing only the modules their application needs.
Understanding the JRE boundary clarifies several practical decisions:
: shipping a full JDK in a production container adds unnecessary tool overhead. A custom JRE image built with
can be 40–80 MB instead of 300+ MB.
that have no place on a production server. A runtime-only image reduces the attack surface.
: a class not found at runtime often means a missing module or an API that was not included in the custom JRE image.
: some Java distributions (e.g. Eclipse Temurin, Azul Zulu, Amazon Corretto) offer both JDK and pre-built JRE packages. Knowing which you need affects licensing and download choices.
: a purpose-built runtime image contains only what the application uses, shrinking container layers and reducing startup time.
: developers work with the JDK; CI/CD and production machines only need the JRE components, enforcing a clean boundary.
: the Java class libraries are extremely stable—APIs added in Java 1.0 still work in Java 21. Code written against the JRE API rarely breaks across upgrades.
: the class libraries abstract OS differences.
works identically on Windows, Linux, and macOS; the JRE handles the OS-specific file-system calls.
No standalone JRE installer from Java 11+
: Oracle removed the separate JRE download. You either ship the full JDK or build a custom image with
. Third-party distributions (Temurin, Zulu) still provide pre-built JRE packages for convenience.
: building a custom JRE image requires identifying all required modules, which can be non-trivial for applications that use reflection or load classes dynamically.

: users may have a different JRE version installed than the one the application was compiled and tested against. Long-term-support (LTS) versions help, but version mismatches still occur in unmanaged environments.
files. Applications that generate and compile code at runtime (e.g. Groovy scripts, some template engines) require either the JDK or the

When a Java application starts, the JVM's Class Loader looks up classes first in the boot class path (the JRE's own libraries), then on the application classpath or module path. The class libraries provide the concrete implementations of every API class:
, and so on are all compiled classes shipped inside the JRE.
In the modular system (Java 9+), the JRE libraries are grouped into named modules under

. Each module declares which packages it exports and which other modules it depends on. The JVM enforces these boundaries at startup—if your code tries to access a package from a module not on the module path, it fails fast with an
Core types — always present, implicitly required
The diagram below shows how JDK, JRE, and JVM nest inside each other, and where each set of components lives:

attrs.alt: JRE vs JDK component breakdown showing JDK contains developer tools plus the JRE, and the JRE contains JVM plus Java class libraries

content.text: . Every JDK installation includes a complete JRE (and JVM); every JRE includes the JVM. You can run bytecode with just the JVM, but you would lose access to the class libraries most code depends on.
Use a JRE-only runtime (or a custom
Production containers and VMs where the artifact is already compiled and only needs to run.
End-user desktops that install a Java-based GUI application (e.g. JetBrains IDEs ship their own bundled JRE).
Size-constrained environments where 300 MB+ JDK footprint is unacceptable.
Development workstations — you need javac, javap, jdb, and the diagnostic tools.
CI/CD build servers where compilation is part of the pipeline.
Applications that compile Java at runtime (Groovy, JSP compilers, annotation processors).
Building a custom JRE with jlink
assembles a minimal runtime image from a set of modules. This is the modern way to create a JRE for production.

# Step 1: discover which modules your application needs
# (run from the root of your compiled module, or against your JAR)
jdeps --print-module-deps --ignore-missing-deps myapp.jar
# Example output: java.base,java.logging,java.net.http,java.sql

# Step 2: build the custom runtime image
jlink \
  --module-path $JAVA_HOME/jmods \
  --add-modules java.base,java.logging,java.net.http,java.sql \
  --output ./runtime \
  --strip-debug \
  --no-header-files \
  --no-man-pages \
  --compress=2

# Step 3: run your application with the custom JRE
./runtime/bin/java -jar myapp.jar

# Compare sizes
du -sh $JAVA_HOME   # ~320 MB (full JDK)
du -sh ./runtime    # ~45 MB (custom JRE)
Embedding a JRE in a Docker image

# Stage 1: build with the JDK
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jdk AS builder
WORKDIR /build
COPY . .
RUN ./mvnw package -DskipTests
# Build a minimal JRE for this specific app
RUN jlink \
    --module-path $JAVA_HOME/jmods \
    --add-modules $(jdeps --print-module-deps --ignore-missing-deps target/myapp.jar) \
    --output /custom-jre \
    --strip-debug --no-header-files --no-man-pages --compress=2

# Stage 2: run with the minimal JRE only — no JDK tools shipped
FROM debian:bookworm-slim
COPY --from=builder /custom-jre /opt/java
COPY --from=builder /build/target/myapp.jar /app/myapp.jar
ENV PATH="/opt/java/bin:$PATH"
EXPOSE 8080
CMD ["java", "-jar", "/app/myapp.jar"]
This two-stage pattern is idiomatic for production Java containers. The final image contains no compiler, no debugger, no JVM source files—only what is needed to execute the packaged application.
Inspecting the runtime version and available modules

javac RuntimeInfo.java && java RuntimeInfo
# Java version : 21.0.3
# JRE home     : /usr/lib/jvm/java-21-openjdk
# Vendor       : Eclipse Adoptium
# java.base
# java.compiler
# java.datatransfer
# java.desktop
# ... (70+ modules in a full JDK)
Detecting whether the current runtime is a JDK or JRE
A SaaS company runs a Spring Boot REST API in Kubernetes. Each pod previously used the official
base image (328 MB). After switching to a custom JRE built with
base, the final image dropped to 78 MB—a 76 % reduction. The modules required were:
module was needed because Spring's reflection-heavy internals use
and a test run against the stripped image. The smaller image also improved pod start time from 8 s to 6 s because Kubernetes had less to pull on cold nodes.
Shipping the full JDK in production containers

# WRONG: ships javac, javap, jdb, jmod — none needed at runtime
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jdk
COPY myapp.jar /app/
CMD ["java", "-jar", "/app/myapp.jar"]

# RIGHT: use JRE image or custom jlink image
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre
COPY myapp.jar /app/
CMD ["java", "-jar", "/app/myapp.jar"]
Missing modules in a jlink image
Forgetting a module produces a cryptic error at runtime, not at image build time:

# At runtime: java.lang.NoClassDefFoundError or
# java.lang.reflect.InaccessibleObjectException
# if java.management or java.naming was omitted

# Fix: always run jdeps on the final fat JAR *before* calling jlink
jdeps --print-module-deps --ignore-missing-deps \
      --class-path 'libs/*' \
      myapp.jar
Confusing java.home across JDK vs JRE installs

Use a JRE or custom jlink image in every production container. Never ship javac or diagnostic tools to end users.
as part of your CI pipeline to detect module dependencies before they become runtime surprises.
Pin a specific JRE version in container images rather than using :latest tags—JRE minor updates can include security fixes that change runtime behaviour.
for additional size reduction. These flags remove debug symbols and apply zip compression to module files.
For maximum startup speed in serverless, evaluate GraalVM Native Image, which converts the JRE + application into a native binary with no JVM warmup at all.
The JRE's class library is optimised to share data across JVM instances via
. CDS pre-processes JRE classes into a shared archive that multiple JVM processes map read-only—reducing per-process memory and improving startup time.

# Generate an application-specific CDS archive (Java 13+)
java -Xshare:dump -XX:SharedArchiveFile=app-cds.jsa \
     -cp myapp.jar com.example.Main

# Use the archive on subsequent starts (~30-50% faster class loading)
java -Xshare:on -XX:SharedArchiveFile=app-cds.jsa \
     -cp myapp.jar com.example.Main

AppCDS (Application Class-Data Sharing) is especially valuable in Kubernetes where many pods run identical JARs—each pod benefits from the pre-processed class archive without paying the class-loading cost repeatedly. Spring Boot 3.3+ generates CDS archives automatically with
Most popular OpenJDK build; Docker official image
Alpine-compatible builds; good for minimal containers
Default on Amazon Linux; long-term patches from AWS
Commercial license for production; free for development

# SDKMAN — manage multiple JDK/JRE versions on one machine
curl -s "https://get.sdkman.io" | bash
sdk install java 21.0.3-tem   # Temurin JDK
sdk install java 21.0.3-zulu  # Zulu JDK (includes JRE builds)
sdk use java 21.0.3-tem       # switch active version

# List all available distributions
sdk list java | grep -E "(tem|zulu|amzn).*21"
The JRE is a critical security boundary. The class library includes the JCE (Java Cryptography Extension), TLS implementation, and the security manager framework. Key security practices:

: Oracle and OpenJDK publish Critical Patch Updates (CPUs) quarterly. JRE vulnerabilities include TLS flaws, deserialization gadgets, and XML parser exploits. Pin to a patched minor version in your Dockerfile FROM line.
: Java object deserialization is a common attack vector. Use
(Java 9+) to allow-list only the classes your application expects to deserialize.
flags unless absolutely necessary. They bypass the module boundary and re-expose internal JRE APIs that may change or have security implications.
: in containers, the JRE process should run as an unprivileged user. The JVM does not require root; running as root expands the blast radius of any runtime vulnerability.

Think of the JRE as the complete factory floor needed to manufacture a product from pre-cut parts. The bytecode (pre-cut parts) arrives already shaped. The JRE provides the assembly line (JVM) and the standard toolbox of components (class libraries) that every worker on that line can reach for. The JDK is the factory floor
the design studio upstairs where new parts (source code) are cut in the first place. You need the full building during design; you only need the factory floor for production runs.

The Java class library is the most portable, battle-tested standard library in existence—30+ years of accretion with near-perfect backwards compatibility. Understanding its major areas helps you write idiomatic Java without reinventing what is already there.
. This package contains the building blocks of the language itself.
— root of every class hierarchy; provides
literals are interned in the string pool; the JVM shares one object per unique literal.
: box primitives for use in collections. Auto-boxing inserts
transparently—at a small allocation cost per boxing operation.
for lightweight virtual threads (Project Loom) that the JVM multiplexes onto OS threads automatically.
The Java Collections Framework provides interfaces (
) and concrete implementations. Choosing the right implementation matters for performance:
I/O and NIO — java.io and java.nio
(Java 7+) brought the modern file API that replaced the error-prone

The JRE exposes runtime state through the
API. These MXBeans are useful for health checks, capacity planning, and adaptive tuning—and are the foundation for monitoring agents like Prometheus JMX Exporter:
The JRE = JVM + Java class libraries. It is everything needed to run compiled Java but nothing for compiling it.
JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM — each is a strict superset of the next smaller component.
Since Java 11 there is no separate JRE installer from Oracle; use jlink to build a custom, minimal runtime image for production.
to identify the exact module set needed.
A jlink image can be 30–80 MB vs 300+ MB for a full JDK, significantly reducing container pull time and attack surface.
AppCDS archives shared between pods can cut class-loading time 30–50% without changing application code.
Patch the JRE quarterly and run as a non-root user in containers—the JRE's TLS and deserialization code are historically common vulnerability targets.
